Final Exam

CautionInstructions (Ignore at your own risk)

General:

  • Submit your responses as a nicely formatted notebook (.ipynb) file.
  • If your answer includes many decimal places (e.g., 3.14159265358979), please round it to a reasonable number of decimals for readability (typically 3 to 4).
  • For ease of marking, avoid showing the outputs of unnecessary values.
  • Make sure your code runs without errors and shows all required outputs.
  • Good coding style matters — clean, organized, and well-commented code will be rewarded. Disorganized, redundant, or poorly structured code may lose marks.

Requirements (IMPORTANT):

  • Unless otherwise specified, set any trimming you need to do at 20% and set \(\alpha = 0.05\).
  • Unless the instructions explicitly state otherwise (e.g., “assume the data are normally distributed”, “use an OLS model”), you are responsible for checking whether the assumptions of the method you employ are reasonable and robust approaches need to be used.
    • If classical test assumptions are not violated, use the classical test.
    • Do not remove outliers unless explicitly asked to.
  • Respect the principle of marginality.

Plots, Packages, and Functions:

  • All plots must be made with ggplot2 and include clear, descriptive axis titles rather than default column names.
  • Only the following packages are permitted: tidyverse, WRS2, car, nlme
  • Unless stated otherwise, The following functions are NOT permitted:
    • IQR, quantile(), mad(), t.test(), yuen(), trimse(), chisq.test()

Question 1

Pet ownership often comes with strong opinions. Some people swear that having a pet photo visible during stressful moments helps them feel calmer, while others think it’s just sentimentality and wishful thinking.

A local psychologist wanted to test whether the type of pet photo and the emotional tone of the photo affect how anxious people feel during a stressful task.

To test this, participants were asked to complete a timed mental arithmetic task in a quiet room while intermittent buzzer sounds played in the background (to create a mildly stressful atmosphere).

The study used a 2 × 3 factorial design with two independent variables:

  • Photo Type: Dog or Cat
  • Photo Tone: Adorable, Neutral, or Intimidating

For the photo, a participant might see, for example, an adorable golden retriever puppy, an intimidating Doberman, a neutral tabby cat, or an intimidating hissing cat. The photo was placed in a frame on the desk where participants could see it throughout the task.

During each session, the psychologist measured mean heart rate (in beats per minute) using a chest-strap monitor. Higher heart rate is presumed to indicate greater physiological arousal/anxiety.

The data can be found in pet_photo_hr.csv.

The researcher wanted to know whether adorable and intimidating photos affect stress differently than neutral photos, and whether this effect depends on the type of animal shown.

Use the pet_photo_hr.csv data, fit a classic OLS ANOVA model that predicts heart rate from both photo type and photo tone, including their interaction. For each main effect and interaction, report the F-ratio, degrees of freedom, and p-value.

# Load Data
pets <- read_csv("data/pet_photo_hr.csv") |>
  arrange(id)

# Factor Data
pets$photo_type <- factor(pets$photo_type)
pets$photo_tone <- factor(
  pets$photo_tone,
  levels = c("Intimidating", "Neutral", "Adorable")
)

# Set Type Contrasts
cats_vs_dogs <- c(1, 0)
contrasts(pets$photo_type) <- cats_vs_dogs

# Set Tone Contrasts
I_vs_N <- c(1, 0, 0)
A_vs_N <- c(0, 0, 1)
contrasts(pets$photo_tone) <- cbind(I_vs_N, A_vs_N)

# Model
pet_mod <- lm(
  hr_bpm ~ photo_type + photo_tone + photo_type:photo_tone,
  data = pets
)

# Check balancing
table(pets$photo_type, pets$photo_tone) # Not balanced
     
      Intimidating Neutral Adorable
  Cat           19      20       19
  Dog           19      18       20
# Type II Sum of Squares (to respect principle of marginality)
library(car)
effects <- Anova(pet_mod, type = "II")
effects
Anova Table (Type II tests)

Response: hr_bpm
                      Sum Sq  Df F value    Pr(>F)    
photo_type            2755.9   1  92.234 3.429e-16 ***
photo_tone             880.5   2  14.735 2.167e-06 ***
photo_type:photo_tone 1751.6   2  29.311 6.513e-11 ***
Residuals             3256.9 109                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Question 2

Calculate an effect size in terms of \(\omega^2\) for each of the previous question’s ANOVA results. Do not round your outputs for this question, display as much precision as possible.

Given this and the previous question, describe your findings of the main effects and interaction.

# Extracting values from ANOVA table
MS_r <- effects$`Sum Sq`[4] / effects$Df[4]
SST <- sum(effects$`Sum Sq`)

# Omega^2 values
(effects$`Sum Sq`[1:3] - effects$Df[1:3] * MS_r) / (SST + MS_r)
[1] 0.31424663 0.09461649 0.19502853

There was a large significant main effect of photo type, a medium-sized main effect of photo tone and a moderate to large interaction between them.


Question 3

Given the previous two questions, create a very professional looking plot (for a scientific article). Save that plot as a high-resolution .png file and upload it alongside your notebook when you submit this exam. The plot should display group means, 95% confidence intervals, and make visualizing any potential interaction easy.

Display a dataframe of the group means and confidence intervals you calculated. Do not include any other stats in this dataframe.

plot_data <- pets |>
  group_by(photo_type, photo_tone) |>
  summarise(
    #.by = c(photo_type, photo_tone),
    n = length(hr_bpm),
    m = mean(hr_bpm),
    se = sd(hr_bpm) / sqrt(n),
    df = n - 1,
    alpha = 0.05,
    t_crit = abs(qt(alpha / 2, df = df)),
    ci_bot = m - t_crit * se,
    ci_top = m + t_crit * se
  ) |>
  select(photo_type, photo_tone, m, ci_bot, ci_top)

plot_data
# A tibble: 6 × 5
# Groups:   photo_type [2]
  photo_type photo_tone          m   ci_bot   ci_top
  <fct>      <fct>           <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 Cat        Intimidating 89.82057 87.43244 92.20871
2 Cat        Neutral      89.95697 87.87189 92.04206
3 Cat        Adorable     92.86431 89.88085 95.84778
4 Dog        Intimidating 89.59796 86.95180 92.24412
5 Dog        Neutral      80.33791 78.04949 82.62633
6 Dog        Adorable     73.56156 70.43933 76.68380
ggplot(
  plot_data,
  aes(
    x = photo_tone,
    y = m,
    group = photo_type,
    shape = photo_type,
    linetype = photo_type
  )
) +
  geom_line(position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_bot, ymax = ci_top),
    width = .2,
    linetype = 1,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(70, 100, 5)) +
  labs(
    x = "Tone",
    y = "Heart Rate (bpm)",
    shape = "Type",
    linetype = "Type"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(colour = "black", size = 12),
    axis.text.y = element_text(colour = "black", size = 13),
    axis.title = element_text(colour = "black", size = 14),
    legend.text = element_text(colour = "black", size = 13),
    legend.title = element_text(colour = "black", size = 14),
  )
  ggsave("perfect_plot.png", dpi = 300, units = "cm", width = 13, height = 9)
Note

Most marks were lost due to readability issues. i.e., your text and visual elements (shapes, lines, data points) were too small to read clearly when the graph was reduced to fit a quarter-page. When designing graphs, ensure all labels, legends, and key features remain legible at smaller scales.


Question 4

Display a table of the planned contrast results tested in your model using the pet_photo_hr.csv dataset. The table must include the following columns: coefficient estimate, standard error, t-value, p-value, and effect size. For each contrast briefly state, in plain English, (1) what comparison was tested, (2) whether the comparison is statistically significant, (3) the direction of the effect, and (4) the size of the effect.

# Note: Contrasts were set back in Question 1.

coef_tab <- as.data.frame(summary(pet_mod)$coefficients)
t_stats <- coef_tab[, 3]
df <- nrow(pets) - 5 - 1

coef_tab$eta_sq <- t_stats^2 / (t_stats^2 + df)
coef_tab$eta <- sqrt(t_stats^2 / (t_stats^2 + df))
coef_tab
                              Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)                  80.337909   1.288397 62.354939 4.290801e-87
photo_type1                   9.619064   1.775933  5.416345 3.665471e-07
photo_toneI_vs_N              9.260050   1.797934  5.150384 1.164147e-06
photo_toneA_vs_N             -6.776344   1.775933 -3.815654 2.257365e-04
photo_type1:photo_toneI_vs_N -9.396450   2.509808 -3.743892 2.911852e-04
photo_type1:photo_toneA_vs_N  9.683683   2.494094  3.882645 1.774751e-04
                                eta_sq       eta
(Intercept)                  0.9727305 0.9862710
photo_type1                  0.2120679 0.4605083
photo_toneI_vs_N             0.1957290 0.4424127
photo_toneA_vs_N             0.1178319 0.3432665
photo_type1:photo_toneI_vs_N 0.1139416 0.3375524
photo_type1:photo_toneA_vs_N 0.1214986 0.3485665

Main Effect of Species:

  • Across all photo tones, cat photos are associated with significantly higher heart rates than dog photos, with a large effect size.

Intimidating vs. neutral tone:

  • Across both animal species, intimidating photos are associated with significantly higher heart rates than neutral photos, with a large effect size.

Adorable vs. Neutral tone:

  • Across both animal species, neutral photos are associated with significantly higher heart rates than adorable photos, with a moderate effect size.

Species \(\times\) (Intimidating vs. Neutral) interaction:

  • The difference in heart rate between intimidating and neutral photos depends significantly on the animal species, with a moderately strong effect. Dog photos tend to show a reduction in heart rate going from intimidating and neutral, whereas cat photos do not.

Species \(\times\) (Adorable vs. Neutral) interaction:

  • The difference in heart rate between neutral and adorable photos depends significantly on the animal species, with a moderately strong effect. Dog photos tend to show a reduction in heart rate going from neutral to adorable, whereas cat photos do not.

Question 5

In the summer of 1975, Steven Spielberg’s blockbuster Jaws terrified moviegoers and fundamentally changed beach culture across America. Coastal communities reported widespread anxiety about shark encounters, even in areas with historically minimal shark activity. Marine biologists and psychologists have long debated whether the film created a “fear amplification effect” that influenced how beachgoers perceived and reported shark-related incidents.

A group of behavioural psychologists and marine scientists has compiled a historical dataset of reported shark sighting incidents from coastal town records spanning 1970–1980 (capturing the years before and after Jaws’ release). The dataset, jaws_fear.csv, contains the following variables:

  • anxiety_score: a continuous composite measure of reported fear/anxiety level during the incident, developed through systematic analysis of witness statements and newspaper reports using validated psychological scaling methods. Higher scores indicate greater distress, with typical values ranging from 30–80, though extreme cases may fall outside this range.

  • water_turbidity: a continuous measure of water clarity at the time of sighting, measured in NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units). Higher values indicate murkier water conditions. Typical coastal readings range from 5–30 NTU, though storm events and other factors can produce substantially higher values

  • viewer_status: categorical variable indicating the reporting witness’s exposure to the film:

    • “Pre-Release” (incident occurred before June 1975),
    • “Non-Viewer” (incident after release, witness had not seen Jaws),
    • “Viewer” (incident after release, witness had seen Jaws).

  • peak_summer: categorical variable (Yes, No) indicating whether the incident occurred during peak beach season (July–August), when Jaws was most heavily promoted and discussed.

The researchers hypothesize that poor water clarity (e.g., high turbidity) may increase anxiety levels during shark sightings, particularly among those who had seen Jaws during peak summer months. They are also interested in whether film exposure moderates the relationship between environmental conditions and perceived anxiety.

Your Task:

a. Data Preperation

  • Remove potential outliers using a robust outlier-detection method.

b. Hierarchical Regression Analysis

Fit four OLS regression models as follows:

  • Model 1: Predict anxiety_score using only the intercept (grand mean model).
  • Model 2: Add water_turbidity as a predictor.
  • Model 3: Add viewer_status as a predictor.
  • Model 4: Add peak_summer as a predictor.

c. Coding Scheme

Set up your categorical variables so that:

  • \(b_2\) represents the effect of being a “Viewer” of the film (compared to “Pre-Release”).
  • \(b_3\) represents the effect of being a “Non-Viewer” of the film (compared to “Pre-Release”).
  • \(b_4\) represents the effect of the incident occurring during peak summer season.

d. Reporting

Report the fitted regression equation for each of the four models.

# Load Data
jaws <- read_csv("data/jaws_fear.csv")

# Remove outliers
madn <- median(abs(jaws$water_turbidity - median(jaws$water_turbidity))) /
  0.6745
outs <- (abs(jaws$water_turbidity - median(jaws$water_turbidity)) / madn) > 2.24
outliers <- jaws[outs, ]
jaws <- jaws[!outs, ]
# Set viewer_status contrasts
jaws$viewer_status <- factor(
  jaws$viewer_status,
  levels = c("Viewer", "Non-Viewer", "Pre-Release")
)

viewer <- c(1, 0, 0)
non_viewer <- c(0, 1, 0)
contrasts(jaws$viewer_status) <- cbind(viewer, non_viewer)
# Set peak_summer Contrasts
jaws$peak_summer <- factor(jaws$peak_summer, levels = c("Yes", "No"))
peak <- c(0, 1)
contrasts(jaws$peak_summer) <- peak
# Models
mod1 <- lm(anxiety_score ~ 1, data = jaws)
mod2 <- lm(anxiety_score ~ water_turbidity, data = jaws)
mod3 <- lm(
  anxiety_score ~ water_turbidity + viewer_status,
  data = jaws
)
mod4 <- lm(
  anxiety_score ~ water_turbidity + viewer_status + peak_summer,
  data = jaws
)

Model 1 (intercept only model): \[ \hat{y} = (53.61) \]

Model 2: \[ \hat{y} = (39.28) + (1.03) x_1 \]

Model 3: \[ \hat{y} = (33.1) + (0.99) x_1 + (17.24) x_2 + (2.88) x_3 \]

Model 4: \[ \hat{y} = (39.2) + (0.99) x_1 + (17.36) x_2 + (2.72) x_3 + (-6.9) x_4 \]

NoteFYI
  • When removing outliers, you are only permitted to do it on the basis of an independent/predictor variable (water_turbidity), not a dependent/response variable (anxiety_score).
  • If you searched for outliers on both continuous variables, you were scored on (amoung other things) if you filtered correctly. E.g., used out_anx == FALSE | out_wat == FALSE, not out_anx == FALSE & out_wat == FALSE.

Question 6

Create a scatterplot of anxiety_score versus water_turbidity that effectively visualizes both the data and any removed outliers.

ggplot(jaws, aes(x = water_turbidity, y = anxiety_score)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_point(
    data = outliers,
    size = 3,
    shape = 4,
    stroke = 1,
    colour = "red"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  scale_y_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  labs(x = "Water Turbidity (NTU)", y = "Anxiety Score")
NoteFYI
  • Using a logarithmic scale helps remove the heavy clustering, thus visualizing the trend more effectively.
  • Outliers should be distinguished by shape or size (not just colour).

Question 7

Consider the outliers identified by your outlier-detection method. Do you believe it is appropriate to remove them from your analysis? Justify your decision.

It is not appropriate to remove these outliers. While they represent extreme values, they appear to follow the same underlying relationship between water turbidity and anxiety score as the rest of the data (i.e., they are not inconsistent with the overall trend). This suggests they are legitimate observations rather than data entry errors or measurement anomalies.

However, these extreme values likely exert substantial leverage and influence on the regression line, potentially distorting coefficient estimates and inflating standard errors.

A better approach would be to retain these observations but use a robust regression method that downweights influential points without discarding valid data.


Question 8

Using the fourth model, answer the following research question: Does being a “Non-Viewer” of Jaws result in a statistically significant difference in anxiety scores compared to incidents that occurred prior to the film’s release (i.e., the “Pre-Release” reference group)?

Report:

  • The size and direction of the effect in the original units.
  • The strength of the association expressed as a partial correlation coefficient.

Display your stats to 16 digits (trailing zeros do not need to be shown).

df <- summary(mod4)$df[2]
b3 <- summary(mod4)$coefficients[4, 1]
t_stat <- summary(mod4)$coefficients[4, 3]
p_val <- summary(mod4)$coefficients[4, 4]
r <- sqrt(t_stat^2 / (t_stat^2 + df))

# Results
# paste0("b_3 = ", print(b3, digits = 16))

print(paste0("b_3 = ", b3), digits = 16)
print(paste0("p   = ", p_val), digits = 16) # Not necessary to show
print(paste0("r   = ", r), digits = 16)
[1] "b_3 = 2.71734613232849"
[1] "p   = 0.00273192813190057"
[1] "r   = 0.166207064547287"

Although the effect size was small, individuals in the Non-Viewer group (those who experienced shark incidents after Jaws was released) reported significantly higher anxiety scores than those in the Pre-Release group, who had encountered incidents before the film’s release when holding other variables constant.


Question 9

Typography experts have long debated whether \(\text{serif}\) fonts (fonts with small decorative strokes at the ends of letters, like \(\text{Times New Roman}\)) or sans-serif fonts (clean fonts without these embellishments, like the horrendous visual abomination that is Calibri, or the sleek and ever-popular Helvetica) lead to faster reading comprehension. Some argue that \(\text{serifs}\) guide the eye along lines of text, while others claim the simplicity of sans-serif fonts reduces visual clutter.

A psychologist decided to test this question empirically. She had participants complete timed reading comprehension tasks of equivalent difficulty. Each task involved reading a 500-word passage and answering 10 comprehension questions as quickly and accurately as possible.

The 500-word passage could be displayed in either Computer Modern Unicode (a \(\text{serif}\) font) or its sans-serif variant (CMU Sans Serif).

The dataset, font_speed.csv, contains the following variables:

  • id: unique identifier for each student.
  • condition: The typeface used.
  • comp_time: The time in seconds to complete the reading task.

The researcher hypothesized that sans-serif fonts produce faster reading speeds than \(\text{serif}\) fonts. Conduct an appropriate statistical test to evaluate whether the data provide sufficient evidence to support the researcher’s hypothesis.

Report all of the following:

  • Null and alternative hypothesis
  • Test statistic
  • Degrees of freedom
  • p-value
  • 95% confidence interval
# Load data (note data file's rows are out of order)
# Note that data is paired
fonts <- read_csv("data/font_speed.csv") |>
  arrange(id)

# Check normality of difference scores
fonts_wide <- fonts |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = condition, values_from = comp_time)

diffs <- fonts_wide$Sans_Serif - fonts_wide$Serif

ggplot(mapping = aes(sample = diffs)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles")

The normality assumption is not reasonable. A (one-sample / paired) trimmed t-test needs to be used on the difference scores.

\(H_0: \mu_\text{sans} - \mu_\text{serif} \geq 0\)

\(H_1: \mu_\text{sans} - \mu_\text{serif} < 0\)

Note

To support the researcher’s hypothesis, it needs to be \(H_1\).

library(WRS2)
# Note: difference scores were calculated above

# Basic stats
N <- length(diffs) # of pairs
G <- 0.2
m_diff <- mean(diffs, tr = G)
s <- sqrt(winvar(diffs, tr = G))
se <- s / ((1 - 2 * G) * sqrt(N))
h <- N - 2 * floor(G * N)
df = h - 1

# Test stat
mu_diff <- 0
t_stat <- (m_diff - mu_diff) / se

# P-value
p <- pt(t_stat, df = df)

# Confidence interval
alpha <- 0.05
t_crit <- abs(qt(alpha, df = df))

low_ci <- m_diff - Inf * se
top_ci <- m_diff + t_crit * se

# Results
paste0("Test Stat = ", round(t_stat, 5))
paste0("df        = ", round(df, 5))
paste0("p         = ", round(p, 5))
paste0("95% CI    = [", round(low_ci, 5), ", ", round(top_ci, 5), "]")
[1] "Test Stat = -3.98248"
[1] "df        = 101"
[1] "p         = 6e-05"
[1] "95% CI    = [-Inf, -5.37416]"

Question 10

A behavioural psychologist wanted to test the partial reinforcement effect: the well-established finding that behaviours learned under intermittent (partial) reinforcement schedules are more resistant to extinction than behaviours learned under continuous reinforcement.

She designed a simple computerized task where participants trained a virtual pet by clicking a button to deliver treats. Participants were randomly assigned to one of four reinforcement schedules during the training phase:

  • Continuous (CRF): The pet responded positively (wagged tail, jumped) after every single treat.
  • Fixed Ratio (FR): The pet responded positively after every 3rd treat only.
  • Variable Ratio (VR): The pet responded positively after an average of every 3rd treat (randomly varying).
  • Random Ratio (RR): The pet responded positively completely at random, unrelated to treat delivery.

After the training phase, all participants entered an extinction phase where the pet stopped responding entirely, no matter how many treats were given. The researcher measured persistence by recording the total number of button clicks participants made during the extinction phase before they voluntarily stopped trying.

The number of extinction clicks was categorized as follows:

  • Low persistence: 0–50 clicks
  • Moderate persistence: 51–100 clicks
  • High persistence: More than 100 clicks

The results are summarized in the following table:

Low persistence (0–50) Moderate persistence (51–100) High persistence (>100)
CRF 65 42 18
FR 25 48 52
VR 19 38 68
RR 15 31 74

Does the type of reinforcement schedule experienced during training significantly influence participants’ persistence behaviour during extinction?

pre <- data.frame(
  low = c(65, 25, 19, 15),
  moderate = c(42, 48, 38, 31),
  high = c(18, 52, 68, 74)
)

rownames(pre) <- c("CRF", "FR", "VR", "RR")

# Get Row, Column Totals, and Overall Total
RowTotal <- apply(pre, MARGIN = 1, FUN = sum)
ColTotal <- apply(pre, MARGIN = 2, FUN = sum)

#Get Total number of Children
N <- sum(RowTotal)

# Get expected Frequencies
expected <- RowTotal %*% t(ColTotal) / N

#Calculate the test-statistic
chiSq <- sum(((pre - expected)^2) / expected)

#Degrees of Freedom
df <- (4 - 1) * (3 - 1)

#p-value
p <- pchisq(chiSq, df = df, lower.tail = FALSE)

# Results
paste0("Test Stat = ", round(chiSq, 5))
paste0("DF = ", df)
paste0("p = ", round(p, 5))
[1] "Test Stat = 90.5954"
[1] "DF = 6"
[1] "p = 0"

Yes, the type of reinforcement schedule does appear to impact their responding (i.e., the amount of clicks is not independent of the schedule).


Question 11

How much more (or less) likely are participants to demonstrate high persistence when trained under a Variable Ratio schedule compared to a Fixed Ratio schedule? Interpret your result in plain language.

odds_vr <- 68 / (19 + 38)
odds_fr <- 52 / (25 + 48)
or <- odds_vr / odds_fr

or
[1] 1.674764

Participants on a Variable Ratio schedule are 1.67 times more likely to high persistence compared to those on a Fixed Ratio schedule.


Question 12

Suppose, instead of categorizing the responses, the researcher analyzed the actual amount of clicks per person made and also assessed the person’s gaming experience, on the assumption that experianced gamers are accustomed to variable reinforcement schedules (e.g., loot boxes, random rewards) in games. The data can be found in extinction.csv.

Appropriately assess whether there are any significant main effects or interactions.

pre <- read_csv("data/extinction.csv")

# Is it balanced?
table(pre$schedule, pre$prior_exp)
     
      Experienced Inexperienced
  CRF          30            30
  FR           30            30
  RR           30            30
  VR           30            30
# Does it satisfy assumptions?
pre$schedule <- factor(pre$schedule)
pre$prior_exp <- factor(pre$prior_exp)

mod <- lm(responses ~ schedule + prior_exp + schedule:prior_exp, data = pre)

ggplot(pre, aes(x = schedule, y = mod$residuals)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 0) +
  facet_wrap(~prior_exp) +
  labs(x = "Schedule of Reinforcement", y = "Residuals")
ggplot(pre, aes(sample = mod$residuals)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles")

The residuals appear heteroscedastic and non-normal. Robust approaches are required.

t2way(
  responses ~ schedule + prior_exp + schedule:prior_exp,
  tr = 0.2,
  data = pre
)
Call:
t2way(formula = responses ~ schedule + prior_exp + schedule:prior_exp, 
    data = pre, tr = 0.2)

                       value p.value
schedule           1946.3855   0.001
prior_exp           380.0811   0.001
schedule:prior_exp   60.1500   0.001

Both main effects and interactions are significant.


Question 13

Ignoring a possible interaction, does the continuous reinforcement schedule produce significantly different extinction persistence (number of clicks) compared to the Random-Ratio schedule? Assuming it is significant, what direction does the effect move?

post_hocs <- mcp2atm(
  responses ~ schedule + prior_exp + schedule:prior_exp,
  tr = 0.2,
  data = pre
)

post_hocs
Call:
mcp2atm(formula = responses ~ schedule + prior_exp + schedule:prior_exp, 
    data = pre, tr = 0.2)

                         psihat   ci.lower   ci.upper p-value
schedule1             -95.83333 -105.81839  -85.84827 0.00000
schedule2            -148.94444 -162.01978 -135.86911 0.00000
schedule3            -186.22222 -204.50512 -167.93932 0.00000
schedule4             -53.11111  -67.80627  -38.41596 0.00000
schedule5             -90.38889 -109.70147  -71.07631 0.00000
schedule6             -37.27778  -58.11755  -16.43801 0.00001
prior_exp1            163.55556  146.76598  180.34513 0.00000
schedule1:prior_exp1  -16.94444  -26.92951   -6.95938 0.00002
schedule2:prior_exp1    2.50000  -10.57533   15.57533 0.60118
schedule3:prior_exp1  -41.77778  -60.06068  -23.49488 0.00000
schedule4:prior_exp1   19.44444    4.74929   34.13960 0.00066
schedule5:prior_exp1  -24.83333  -44.14591   -5.52075 0.00110
schedule6:prior_exp1  -44.27778  -65.11755  -23.43801 0.00000
post_hocs$contrasts
                  schedule1 schedule2 schedule3 schedule4 schedule5 schedule6
CRF_Experienced           1         1         1         0         0         0
CRF_Inexperienced         1         1         1         0         0         0
FR_Experienced           -1         0         0         1         1         0
FR_Inexperienced         -1         0         0         1         1         0
RR_Experienced            0        -1         0        -1         0         1
RR_Inexperienced          0        -1         0        -1         0         1
VR_Experienced            0         0        -1         0        -1        -1
VR_Inexperienced          0         0        -1         0        -1        -1
                  prior_exp1 schedule1:prior_exp1 schedule2:prior_exp1
CRF_Experienced            1                    1                    1
CRF_Inexperienced         -1                   -1                   -1
FR_Experienced             1                   -1                    0
FR_Inexperienced          -1                    1                    0
RR_Experienced             1                    0                   -1
RR_Inexperienced          -1                    0                    1
VR_Experienced             1                    0                    0
VR_Inexperienced          -1                    0                    0
                  schedule3:prior_exp1 schedule4:prior_exp1
CRF_Experienced                      1                    0
CRF_Inexperienced                   -1                    0
FR_Experienced                       0                    1
FR_Inexperienced                     0                   -1
RR_Experienced                       0                   -1
RR_Inexperienced                     0                    1
VR_Experienced                      -1                    0
VR_Inexperienced                     1                    0
                  schedule5:prior_exp1 schedule6:prior_exp1
CRF_Experienced                      0                    0
CRF_Inexperienced                    0                    0
FR_Experienced                       1                    0
FR_Inexperienced                    -1                    0
RR_Experienced                       0                    1
RR_Inexperienced                     0                   -1
VR_Experienced                      -1                   -1
VR_Inexperienced                     1                    1

Yes, the effect is significant. Random ratio-schedules generate significantly more persistance (see 2nd row of post_hocs labelled “schedule2”).


Question 14

Does the difference between the continuous reinforcement schedule and random-ratio schedule change significantly according to the experiance of the gamers?

No. Between the CRF and RR schedule, there is no significant change in persistance according to the person’s gaming experiance (see 9th row of post_hocs labelled schedule2:prior_exp1).


Question 15

Among the most pressing questions science has yet to answer is whether cone type significantly affects the melting rate of ice cream when all other factors (ice cream type, scoop size, environmental conditions) are held constant. The data ice_cream_melt.csv examines this.

The independent variable is cone type, which has four levels: waffle cone, sugar cone, wafer cone, and pretzel cone. The dependent variable is melting time, measured in minutes from initial placement until the ice cream has completely melted. The tests were conducted in real-world outdoor summer conditions. To control for environmental variability, each of the outdoor testing locations used evaluated all four cone types. This means every location serves as its own control, which helps eliminate confounding variables like slight differences in sunlight angle, wind speed, or surface temperature between locations.

At each testing location, all four cone types were evaluated once. The researchers placed 100g scoops of vanilla ice cream on each cone type and measured how long it takes to melt completely (defined as having a internal temperature of 4°C throughout).

The researchers hypothesize that wafer cones (the dry flaky kind McDonald’s uses for their soft-serve ice-cream) slow the melting rate of ice cream more than each of the other three (more tasty) competitors. Conduct an appropriate hypothesis test(s) to compare melting times across cone types.

library(nlme)

melt <- read_csv("data/ice_cream_melt.csv") |>
  arrange(location)

# Standardize the effect
melt$melt_time_min <- scale(melt$melt_time_min)

# Factor data
melt$location <- factor(melt$location)

melt$cone_type <- factor(
  melt$cone_type,
  levels = c("pretzel", "sugar", "waffle", "wafer")
)

# Set Contrasts
pretz_vs_waf <- c(1, 0, 0, 0)
sugar_vs_waf <- c(0, 1, 0, 0)
waffl_vs_waf <- c(0, 0, 1, 0)
contrasts(melt$cone_type) <- cbind(pretz_vs_waf, sugar_vs_waf, waffl_vs_waf)

# Check orthogonality
t(contrasts(melt$cone_type)) %*% contrasts(melt$cone_type)
             pretz_vs_waf sugar_vs_waf waffl_vs_waf
pretz_vs_waf            1            0            0
sugar_vs_waf            0            1            0
waffl_vs_waf            0            0            1
# Full Model
melt_mod <- lme(melt_time_min ~ cone_type, random = ~ 1 | location, method = "ML", data = melt)

# Check assumptions
ggplot(melt, aes(x = fitted(melt_mod), y = resid(melt_mod))) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 3) +
  labs(title = "Linearity", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals")
ggplot(melt, aes(x = fitted(melt_mod), y = resid(melt_mod))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ location) +
  labs(title = "Independence Check: Residuals vs Fitted by Location", x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals")
ggplot(melt, aes(sample = resid(melt_mod))) +
  stat_qq(size = 2) +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Normality of the Residuals",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  )
ggplot(mapping = aes(sample = ranef(melt_mod)[, 1])) +
  stat_qq(size = 2) +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Normality of the Intercepts",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  )
summary(melt_mod)$tTable
                           Value Std.Error DF    t-value      p-value
(Intercept)            0.6264536 0.1766865 69   3.545565 7.087503e-04
cone_typepretz_vs_waf -1.1953570 0.1008474 69 -11.853128 2.657220e-18
cone_typesugar_vs_waf -1.0844802 0.1008474 69 -10.753677 2.142389e-16
cone_typewaffl_vs_waf -0.2259774 0.1008474 69  -2.240786 2.826149e-02

(Note that these \(b\) coefficients show standard (i.e., z) scores)


Question 16

Regardless of whether your result was significant or not, would you classify the size of the effect for wafer cones compared to the other cone types as conventionally small, medium, or large? Explain your reasoning.

Melt-times were standardized (converted to z-scores) so that differences could be expressed in standard deviations, making them directly comparable to Cohen’s d. When wafer cones were compared to pretzel and sugar cones, large significant differences emerged (approximately -1.2 and -1.08 standard deviation difference respectively). By contrast, the difference between wafer and waffle cones, while still statistically significant, represented a conventionally small effect size (approximately -0.23 standard deviations).
